skill.name: plannotator
skill.description: Interactive plan and diff review for AI coding agents. It opens a browser UI for annotating plans and git diffs, then returns structured feedback to the agent.
skill.summary: Review, annotate, approve, or request changes on AI agent plans and code diffs.
skill.author: supercent-io

security_audit.summary: Static findings are largely true positives for a high-risk integration skill: it installs external software, writes persistent AI-agent hooks, and modifies hidden home-directory configuration files. I found no evidence of malicious prompt injection, but the curl-to-shell installer, persistent command hooks, and shell profile writes require human review before publishing.

high_findings.title: Remote Installer Executes Downloaded Shell Code
high_findings.description: TRUE_POSITIVE: The install script pipes https://plannotator.ai/install.sh directly into bash and suggests a PowerShell irm pipe to iex on Windows. This pattern executes unaudited remote code with the user privileges of the shell running the installer.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The dangerous pattern is explicit at the cited lines and directly executes network-fetched installer content. The only uncertainty is the content served by plannotator.ai, which is outside this repository.

high_findings.title: Persistent AI Agent Hooks Execute plannotator
high_findings.description: TRUE_POSITIVE: Setup scripts write Claude, Gemini, and Codex configuration under hidden home directories so agent workflows automatically run plannotator commands during plan review. This is intended behavior, but it creates persistent command execution inside trusted agent configuration.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: Multiple scripts intentionally create or modify agent configuration files and add commands that invoke plannotator. The behavior is documented, but the persistence and command execution risk are confirmed.

high_findings.title: Agent Slash Commands Invoke Local Shell Commands
high_findings.description: TRUE_POSITIVE: The OpenCode setup writes slash command files containing shell-style command invocations for plannotator review and annotation. If installed, these commands become part of the agent workflow and can execute local tools.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The script clearly writes OpenCode command files with command invocations. Risk depends on user installation and OpenCode command handling, so confidence is high but not absolute.

medium_findings.title: Shell Profile Writes Use Unvalidated Interactive Values
medium_findings.description: TRUE_POSITIVE: configure-remote.sh appends user-provided port, browser, and share URL values to the detected shell profile. The port is unquoted and quoted string values are not escaped, so a malicious or malformed value could affect later shell startup.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The write path and unvalidated values are visible in the script. Exploitation requires a user to enter or pass unsafe values, so the severity is moderate rather than critical.

medium_findings.title: Hidden Home Directory Configuration Access
medium_findings.description: TRUE_POSITIVE: The setup and status scripts read and write hidden home-directory files for Claude, Gemini, Codex, OpenCode, and shell profiles. This access is legitimate for integration setup but security-sensitive because these files influence future agent behavior.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The scanner findings are confirmed by direct home-directory config paths and writes. The behavior is purposeful setup logic, so it is a controlled but real risk.

medium_findings.title: External Service Dependence and Share URL Configuration
medium_findings.description: TRUE_POSITIVE: The skill references plannotator.ai for installation and default sharing behavior, and lets users configure a custom PLANNOTATOR_SHARE_URL. This may send plans, diffs, or review metadata to external services depending on the installed CLI behavior.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: Repository scripts confirm external installation and share URL configuration. The exact data sent by the external CLI is not present in this repository, so the data exposure risk requires human review.

low_findings.title: Benign Command Substitutions in Status and Review Scripts
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE: Several static command-substitution hits collect local status, paths, git branch names, or command versions. These uses are mostly quoted or status-only and do not show direct user-controlled shell construction.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines run standard local discovery commands and git status checks. I did not find evidence that these substitutions execute attacker-provided shell fragments.

low_findings.title: Documentation URL and Backtick Matches Are Mostly Static Text
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE: Many SKILL.md and SKILL.toon hits are examples, documentation, or command snippets rather than code executed by the skill file itself. They remain relevant user instructions, but they are not direct execution paths until a user runs the scripts or commands.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The files contain instructional text and examples, not executable script bodies. The underlying commands may still be risky, but these specific static matches are documentation-level evidence.

dangerous_patterns.title: Pipe to Shell Installer
dangerous_patterns.description: The installer executes a script fetched over HTTPS directly through bash, and the Windows instructions recommend the analogous PowerShell pattern.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The exact pipe-to-shell and irm-to-iex patterns are present on the cited lines.

dangerous_patterns.title: Persistent Agent Configuration Writes
dangerous_patterns.description: Setup scripts create or modify configuration files that control future AI agent behavior and add plannotator command execution.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The scripts directly create or update persistent configuration locations and command strings.

dangerous_patterns.title: Shell Profile Append
dangerous_patterns.description: The remote configuration script appends generated export lines into the detected shell profile.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The generated lines and final append to the shell profile are visible. The main risk comes from unvalidated values persisted into startup files.

content.user_title: Review AI Plans and Diffs Visually
content.value_statement: AI coding agents can start work from incomplete or risky plans. This skill adds a visual review loop for plans and diffs before work continues.
actual_capabilities: Launches plannotator for browser-based plan review before implementation.
Opens a visual git diff review flow for uncommitted changes, commits, or branch ranges.
Adds optional Claude Code, Gemini CLI, Codex CLI, and OpenCode integration scripts.
Supports annotation actions such as delete, insert, replace, and comment.
Provides status checks for installed CLI, hooks, environment variables, and git state.
Documents optional Obsidian and Bear Notes export workflows.
limitations: The skill depends on the external plannotator CLI and service installation flow.
Several setup scripts modify user home directory configuration files.
Remote review and sharing behavior depends on local ports, browser access, and user environment variables.
It does not independently verify that plannotator.ai installer contents are safe.

use_cases.title: Review an implementation plan before coding
use_cases.description: Open the proposed plan in a browser, annotate weak steps, and send structured feedback before implementation starts.
use_cases.target_user: Software engineer using an AI coding agent

use_cases.title: Annotate a code diff after agent changes
use_cases.description: Review a git diff visually, mark exact lines that need changes, and return focused feedback to the agent.

use_cases.title: Set up plan review in remote environments
use_cases.description: Configure fixed ports and environment variables for SSH, WSL, or devcontainer workflows.
use_cases.target_user: Developer working in remote development sessions

prompt_templates.title: Review a simple plan
prompt_templates.prompt: Use plannotator to review this implementation plan before coding. Ask me to approve or request changes after annotation.

prompt_templates.title: Review current changes
prompt_templates.scenario: Intermediate code review after edits exist
prompt_templates.prompt: Open plannotator code review for my current git changes. Summarize the review feedback before making fixes.

prompt_templates.title: Configure remote review
prompt_templates.prompt: Set up plannotator for remote use with a fixed port. Show me the files and environment variables that will change first.

prompt_templates.title: Run a full agent feedback loop
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced plan, implementation, and post-change review
prompt_templates.prompt: Use plannotator to review the plan, wait for approval, implement only approved steps, then run a diff review and address feedback.

output_examples.input: A proposed migration plan needs review before database changes begin.
output_examples.output: The browser review highlights risky migration ordering, missing rollback steps, and acceptance criteria to add before approval.

output_examples.input: A feature branch contains uncommitted changes from an AI agent.
output_examples.output: The diff review identifies exact lines needing test coverage, naming changes, and simpler error handling.

output_examples.input: A developer works through SSH and needs plan review in a local browser.
output_examples.output: Remote mode is configured with a fixed port and clear port-forwarding instructions for browser access.

best_practices: Review setup scripts before running them because they modify home directory configuration files.
Use dry-run options where available before installing hooks or changing agent settings.
Keep each annotation focused on one requested change with clear acceptance criteria.
anti_patterns: Do not pipe remote installer scripts into a shell without reviewing the downloaded content first.
Do not enable every agent integration unless each tool is actively used and trusted.
Do not expose review ports or share URLs without checking the remote access configuration.

faq.question: What does this skill add to an AI coding workflow?
faq.answer: It adds a human review checkpoint for plans and diffs before an agent continues work.

faq.question: Which AI tools does it document?
faq.answer: The skill documents Claude, Claude Code, Codex CLI, Gemini CLI, and OpenCode workflows.

faq.question: Does it modify local files?
faq.answer: Yes. Setup scripts can write hook, prompt, plugin, and shell profile configuration files under the user home directory.

faq.question: Does it use network access?
faq.answer: Yes. The installer downloads from plannotator.ai, and sharing behavior references plannotator-hosted URLs.

faq.question: Can it review code changes?
faq.answer: Yes. The review script opens plannotator against current git changes, a commit, or a branch range.

faq.question: What should users verify first?
faq.answer: Users should inspect installer and setup scripts, then run dry-run modes before enabling persistent hooks.
